failure_surface: Agent failed twice due to: (1) attempting to parse header row 'Date' as datetime data causing DateParseError; (2) using incorrect file path causing FileNotFoundError
root_cause: When using pd.read_excel(), the agent did not consistently separate header rows from data rows before type conversion operations. Additionally, file paths were modified between execution attempts without verification.
minimal_fix_hypothesis: After loading with pandas, explicitly drop header row if present, validate all file paths before opening, and document the offset between Excel 1-indexed rows and DataFrame 0-indexed rows when writing back via openpyxl.
evidence: DateParseError: Unknown datetime string format, unable to parse: Date, at position 0 - header text parsed as datetime data
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/mnt/data/spreadsheet/1_38823_init.xlsx' - inconsistent path between openpyxl load and pandas read
Successful third attempt dropped first row after reading and used correct consistent path: /mnt/data/spreadsheet/38823/1_38823_init.xlsx
Agent successfully converted df.columns = df.iloc[0]; df = df.drop(0).reset_index(drop=True) pattern in final attempt

memory_items.title: Header Row Separation When Mixing Tools
memory_items.lesson: When loading workbooks with pandas for calculation while also using openpyxl for cell-level access, always verify whether pandas treated the first row as headers or data. Explicitly separate them before type conversions.
memory_items.generalization: Always check if header values appear in DataFrame data after pd.read_excel(). If headers become data rows, drop them before applying dtype conversions like pd.to_datetime().

memory_items.title: File Path Consistency Across Tool Usage
memory_items.lesson: When using both pandas and openpyxl on the same workbook, use identical file paths for both libraries. Changing paths mid-trajectory risks FileNotFoundError or working on different files.
memory_items.generalization: Store the source workbook path in a single variable and reference it consistently across all library calls (pandas.read_excel, openpyxl.load_workbook) within the same execution context.

memory_items.title: Row Index Alignment Between Excel and DataFrame
memory_items.lesson: Excel uses 1-based row indexing while pandas uses 0-based indexing. When writing results from a DataFrame back to specific Excel rows via openpyxl, account for this offset correctly.
memory_items.generalization: Document the relationship between sheet_row (1-indexed) and DataFrame index (0-indexed). If header occupies row 1 in Excel, data starts at sheet_row 2 which maps to DataFrame index 0.

edits.content:
### 7. Mixed Tool Execution Hygiene
- **Header/Data Separation:** When combining pandas calculations with openpyxl cell-level access, verify whether the first row was treated as headers or data. Drop header rows from DataFrame before applying dtype conversions to avoid parsing errors on header text.
- **Path Consistency:** Store the input workbook path once and reuse it across all library calls (pandas.read_excel, openpyxl.load_workbook) to prevent FileNotFoundError or unintended file divergence.
- **Row Index Mapping:** Excel uses 1-based row numbering while DataFrames use 0-based indexing. Document the offset clearly when writing DataFrame results back to specific Excel rows via openpyxl.cell().
